title: Vulnerable Driver Written to Disk
description: A process wrote a kernel driver to disk that has been deemed vulnerable. Attackers abuse vulnerable drivers (Bring-Your-Own-Vulnerable-Driver) to gain privileged kernel access, disable security tooling, or escalate. The write itself is not proof of malice; this procedure separates malicious BYOVD from legitimate software that happens to ship a vulnerable driver.

intake.title: Intake and capture core facts
intake.instructions:
Confirm this is a new detection (not a duplicate of an open case) and record the identifiers you will reason about throughout.

- Note the host: identifier, primary user/owner, and business role if known.
- Note the driver file: filename, full path on disk, and file hash.
- Note the writing process: the process that wrote the driver, its full path, and its parent process.
- Record the detection timestamp and the reporting sensor/source.
- Capture the account context the writing process ran under (user vs. SYSTEM/service).

enrich-driver.title: Enrich the driver file
enrich-driver.instructions:
Build a reputation and identity picture for the driver itself, independent of how it arrived.

- Look up the driver hash in a threat-intel / reputation capability; note prevalence (common vs. rare in the wild) and any malware/campaign associations.
- Determine the driver's identity: what product or vendor it legitimately belongs to, and which known vulnerability makes it flagged (the vulnerable driver, not necessarily malicious).
- Check the digital signature: is it validly signed, by whom, and is the signing certificate revoked or expired.
- Note whether this hash/driver appears on any known vulnerable-driver blocklist.

enrich-writer.title: Analyze the writing process and its tree
enrich-writer.instructions:
Understand what wrote the driver and whether that origin is legitimate.

- Walk the full process tree: what launched the writing process, and what (if anything) it spawned afterward.
- Classify the writer: known installer/updater, software-deployment/management agent, a user-launched application, a browser or archive tool (download origin), or an unknown/suspicious binary (script host, LOLBin, renamed, unusual path).
- For the writing process binary, assess its own reputation and signature.
- Establish provenance of the driver on disk: was it dropped by an installer, extracted from an archive, downloaded via browser, or written directly by the flagged process.

writer-malicious?.title: Is the writing process itself clearly malicious or highly suspicious?
writer-malicious?.instructions: Judge the writer independent of the driver. Treat as malicious/suspicious: unsigned or masqueraded binaries, execution from temp/user-writable/unusual paths, script hosts or LOLBins dropping a kernel driver, a tree rooted in phishing or exploited services, or writer hashes with known-bad reputation. Treat as not-obviously-malicious: recognized installers/updaters, deployment agents, and reputable signed applications.

options.label: Yes — writer is malicious / highly suspicious
options.note: Unsigned, masqueraded, LOLBin/script origin, or known-bad.

options.label: No / not obviously — writer looks like legitimate software
options.note: Installer, updater, deployment agent, or reputable app.

attribute-source.title: Attribute the legitimate source
attribute-source.instructions:
The writer looks legitimate; determine which benign scenario this is, because the disposition differs.

- Correlate with change/deployment activity: does a software-deployment or patch system show a matching push to this host at this time (IT-sourced)?
- Determine if the driver was bundled with legitimate software the user installed or that ships on the standard image (bundled-with-legit-software).
- Determine if this is a user-initiated download/install of a real application that simply carries an old/vulnerable driver version (benign but outdated).
- Confirm the user/owner's account of the activity where feasible, and whether the installing action was expected for that host's role.

loaded-check-benign.title: Check whether the driver was actually loaded
loaded-check-benign.instructions:
Even for a legitimate source, a loaded vulnerable driver is a standing exposure. Establish load state before choosing a disposition.

- Determine whether the driver was loaded into the kernel or only written to disk.
- If loaded, note when, by what, and whether it remains loaded now.
- Note whether the host's platform vulnerable-driver blocklist / protections are enabled (would the load even succeed).

evidence.label: Loaded into kernel? (when / by what / still loaded)

benign-branch?.title: Which legitimate scenario applies?
benign-branch?.instructions: Select the attribution you established. This routes to the correct close-out, each with a different follow-up. If evidence does not actually support a benign scenario, go back and re-run the writer judgment.

options.label: Legit app, outdated/vulnerable driver version, no malicious intent
options.note: Real user install; just an old driver.

close-it-sourced.title: Close — sanctioned IT deployment
close-it-sourced.instructions:
Attributed to a sanctioned deployment. Close cleanly and drive down the standing exposure.

- Document the matching deployment/change record as the benign rationale.
- Flag the vulnerable driver to the owning team so the deployment package can be updated or the driver blocked at scale.
- If the driver is loaded and exploitable, recommend remediation (update/remove) even though the source is trusted.
- Propose tuning/suppression scoped to this deployment so sanctioned pushes don't re-alert.
close-it-sourced.summary: Vulnerable driver traced to a sanctioned IT deployment; benign. Flagged for package update / blocklisting and tuning proposed.

close-bundled.title: Close — bundled with legitimate software
close-bundled.instructions:
The driver ships with a sanctioned/known application. Close and reduce future noise.

- Document the parent application and that the driver is a known component of it.
- Note the standing risk: a legitimately-installed vulnerable driver is still abusable, so record whether the fleet should move to a fixed version or blocklist the driver.
- Propose tuning scoped to this application/driver pairing.
close-bundled.summary: Vulnerable driver is a known component of legitimate bundled software; benign. Standing-exposure note filed and tuning proposed.

close-outdated.title: Close — legitimate app, outdated driver, no malicious intent
close-outdated.instructions:
A real user install of a legitimate application carrying an old/vulnerable driver version, with no sign of malicious intent. Close with a hygiene follow-up.

- Document the application, the outdated driver version, and the absence of malicious behavior.
- Recommend updating the application/driver to a non-vulnerable version, or removing it if unneeded.
- If organizationally warranted, raise for vulnerable-driver blocklisting so the old version can't be abused fleet-wide.
close-outdated.summary: Legitimate application with an outdated vulnerable driver; no malicious intent. Update/removal recommended.

loaded-check-mal.title: Determine driver load state and abuse indicators
loaded-check-mal.instructions:
The writer is malicious/suspicious. Establish whether the vulnerable driver was loaded and whether it has been abused, as this drives containment urgency.

- Determine whether the driver was loaded into the kernel and whether it remains loaded.
- Look for BYOVD abuse behavior around the load: security tooling being tampered with or stopped, protected-process access, driver-service creation, or kernel-level actions.
- Identify any privilege escalation or follow-on activity by the writer's tree (new persistence, credential access, lateral movement attempts).
- Preserve volatile evidence (running processes, loaded modules, memory artifacts) before it is lost.

evidence.label: BYOVD abuse indicators (tamper / PPL / kernel actions)

contain.title: Contain the host
contain.instructions:
Credible malicious BYOVD activity. Contain before eradication and handoff.

- Isolate the affected host from the network per policy while preserving investigative access.
- Block the malicious driver and writer by hash across the fleet, and unload the driver where supported.
- Disable or quarantine any malicious service/persistence created around the driver.
- Record every containment action and its timestamp.

scope.title: Scope the blast radius
scope.instructions:
Determine how far the activity reached before handoff.

- Search the fleet for the same driver hash and writer hash on other hosts.
- Build a timeline from first-seen to latest activity across affected hosts.
- Assess what the compromised host/account could reach (data, credentials, adjacent systems).
- Note any evidence of successful privilege escalation or lateral movement identified during containment.

escalate.title: Escalate to incident response
escalate.instructions:
Confirmed malicious BYOVD with scope and initial containment established. Hand off with a complete package.

- Ensure host, driver, writer, load/abuse indicators, timeline, affected assets, and containment actions are all recorded above.
- Assign incident severity per your matrix and notify the on-call IR owner.
- Confirm volatile evidence was preserved before any remediation that would destroy it.
- Transfer ownership; eradication and recovery proceed under the IR playbook.
escalate.summary: Confirmed malicious vulnerable-driver (BYOVD) activity; host contained, scoped, and escalated to IR.
